claims.text: Raymond Moody coined the term 'near-death experience' with his 1975 book 'Life After Life', which sold 13 million copies based on interviews with over a hundred resuscitated patients.

evidence.quote:
1975. Raymond Moody published life
after life. He coined the term
near-death experience. The book sold 13
million copies

claims.rationale: Moody's 1975 book 'Life After Life' is well-documented as the origin of the term 'near-death experience' in popular and academic usage. Sales figures and interview methodology are matters of public record.

claims.text: Classical NDE skeptics explain the phenomenon away as oxygen deprivation causing tunnel vision, endorphins creating peace, and temporal-lobe stress generating visions of dead relatives.

evidence.quote:
Skeptics had answers for all of it.
Oxygen deprivation caused tunnel vision.
Endorphins flood the brain, creating a
sense of peace. The temporal lobe under
stress creates visions of dead
relatives

claims.rationale: Host lays these out as the mainstream skeptical toolkit. These mechanisms are real and are invoked in neuroscience literature (e.g., Blanke on temporal-parietal junction), though whether they fully explain NDEs is disputed. Host accepts them as applicable to individual-witness cases.

claims.text: Shared death experiences (SDEs) occur when healthy, fully conscious bystanders at a deathbed report the same tunnel, light, and deceased relatives that the dying person experiences.

evidence.quote:
These weren't people who died
and come back. These were people who'd
been sitting at someone's bedside when
the person passed and experienced the
exact same thing

claims.rationale: The phenomenon is well-documented in the NDE/SDE research literature (Moody, Fenwick, Peters). Host presents bystander testimony as consistent across hundreds of cases. directTruth moderately above 0.5 because the reports are real and reproducible; what they mean (afterlife vs shared hallucination) is the contested part.

claims.text: After 50 years of NDE research, Raymond Moody said shared death experiences — not NDEs — finally convinced him the afterlife is real.

evidence.quote:
50 years of research, Raymond
Moody said it was shared death
experiences that finally convinced him
the afterlife is real

claims.rationale: Moody's personal conclusion after witnessing an SDE at his own mother's death is documented in his later writings ('Glimpses of Eternity', 2010). That he said it is well-supported; whether his conclusion is correct is what the episode debates. directTruth high on the attribution itself.

dependencies.rationale: Moody's personal case is the paradigm SDE — a family event that convinced the field's own founder.

claims.text: William Peters' Shared Crossing Project has collected over 800 SDE cases, 64% of which are 'remote' — the bystander wasn't in the same room or even the same state as the dying person.

evidence.quote:
64% of the
STDEEs Peters collected were remote. The
person having the experience wasn't at

claims.rationale: Peters founded Shared Crossing in 2013 and published the 2021 peer-reviewed study; the 64%-remote figure appears in his research. Statistical claim drawn directly from his dataset, verifiable via the 2021 AJHPM paper.

claims.text: Peters' 2021 American Journal of Hospice and Palliative Medicine study of 164 SDEs from 107 people was the first peer-reviewed study focused specifically on shared death experiences.
entities: organization:journal of hospice and paliotative medicine

evidence.quote:
first peer-reviewed
study focused specifically on shared
death experiences. They analyzed 164
STEEs from 107 people

claims.rationale: The 2021 AJHPM paper by Peters et al. is publicly indexed and matches all details the host cites: cohort size, journal, first-of-its-kind framing.

claims.text: 87% of SDE experiencers became convinced of an afterlife, their fear of death vanished, and their grief was transformed rather than erased.

evidence.quote:
87% of experiencers became
convinced of an afterlife

claims.rationale: The 87% figure is consistent with Peters' published data on post-SDE attitude shifts. The psychological transformation (belief in afterlife, reduced death anxiety) is the most robust finding even among skeptics — the subjective impact is real whatever the metaphysics.

claims.text: Pim van Lommel's 2001 Lancet study of 344 cardiac-arrest survivors concluded NDEs occurred during clinical death when there was no heartbeat, no brain activity, and no blood flow — the opposite of what neuroscience predicts.

evidence.quote:
These experiences occurred during a
period of clinical death. No heartbeat,
no brain activity, no blood flow. The
clarity of consciousness was inversely

claims.rationale: Van Lommel's 2001 Lancet study is real and widely cited — the finding that 18% of cardiac-arrest survivors reported NDEs is peer-reviewed. Van Lommel's interpretation (that consciousness survives without brain activity) is contested; the raw data is not.

claims.text: Sam Parnia's 2023 Aware 2 study found organized gamma-wave brain activity up to an hour after the heart stopped in 567 cardiac-arrest patients.

evidence.quote:
gamma waves associated with higher
consciousness up to an hour after the
heart stopped

claims.rationale: Parnia's Aware 2 results are published (Resuscitation, 2023) and the gamma-wave observation matches the paper. 'Up to an hour' is a generous reading of the published transient-recovery windows, which are typically shorter; host slightly oversells. Core finding of post-arrest organized brain activity is supported.

claims.text: A 26-year-old nonverbal woman, Anna Katarina Ehrler, who never spoke a word in her life, spontaneously began singing in the half hour before her death, witnessed by multiple staff.

evidence.quote:
She never spoke a
single word in her entire life. In the
half hour before her death, she
spontaneously began singing. Multiple
staff members witnessed this

claims.rationale: Nahm and Greyson published the terminal-lucidity case study in 2014 — the case is real and peer-reviewed. The 'a brain that never worked right suddenly worked perfectly' framing is the authors' interpretation, not a proven mechanism; the observation itself is well-attested.

claims.text: SDE reports are cross-culturally consistent — Hindu and Christian patients describe the same tunnel/light/beings/peace structure regardless of their religious expectations.

evidence.quote:
Hindu patients
didn't always see Hindu deities

claims.rationale: Osis and Haraldsson's 1970s US-India deathbed study is real and found convergent structure across faiths. The claim is peer-reviewed. Critics note that fine-grained content does correlate with culture (which Hindu vs Christian 'beings' appear), so the host's version slightly overstates the uniformity.

claims.text: Children as young as four describe SDE visions of dead relatives they've never met or seen photos of — including physically accurate details like a grandfather's specific scar.

evidence.quote:
four-year-old described her dead
grandfather down to the scar on his left
hand

claims.rationale: Host uses this as the strongest case against cultural-expectation explanations. The specific 'scar on the left hand' detail is cited by Peters but not independently verifiable in-episode. Anecdotal pediatric reports are compelling but vulnerable to parental priming and retrospective confirmation. directTruth below 0.5 pending stronger published documentation.

claims.text: Tibetan Buddhists have a thousand-year-old practice called phowa in which the living guide the dying through the same tunnel, light, and encounters that Western SDE research describes.

evidence.quote:
Tibetan Buddhists
have a practice called pawa

claims.rationale: Phowa is a genuine Tibetan Buddhist transference-of-consciousness practice documented in Bardo Thödol and multiple Vajrayana sources. The host's specific claim that it matches SDE phenomenology is a selective reading — the Tibetan account is much richer and structurally different. Omitting directTruth because the mapping is interpretive.

claims.text: Physicist Sean Carroll argues that life after death is incompatible with everything modern physics knows about atoms and consciousness — when we die, it's like a candle being blown out.

evidence.quote:
Sha Carol argues that life
after death is incompatible with
everything we know about modern physics.
We're made of atoms. So when we die,
it's like a candle being blown out

claims.rationale: Sean Carroll has publicly made this argument on multiple occasions (e.g., his Quanta essay and his 'Core Theory' talks) — attribution and framing are accurate. The truth-value of the metaphysical claim itself is contested; the sociological claim that Carroll argues it is solid.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Carroll's particle-physics argument directly denies the afterlife that Moody's SDE-driven conclusion affirms — two mutually exclusive metaphysical positions framed in the same episode.

[alternative] If consciousness strictly supervenes on atomic structure, van Lommel's 'consciousness without brain activity' interpretation is physically impossible — Carroll's position denies van Lommel's interpretive layer.

claims.text: Classical skeptical explanations (shared psychosis, memory reconstruction, confirmation bias, DMT release) cannot account for remote SDEs, multi-witness bedside cases, cross-cultural consistency, or young children describing never-seen dead relatives.
claims.rationale: Host's central argumentative thesis: the ensemble of SDE phenomena exceeds the explanatory range of any single skeptical mechanism. Each sub-claim (remote cases, children, cross-cultural consistency) has been peer-reviewed. The inferential step from 'classical skeptical models insufficient' to 'therefore afterlife' is what remains contested — so directTruth moderately above 0.5.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] Each classical skeptical mechanism (oxygen deprivation etc.) presupposes the experiencer was themselves dying; remote SDEs and multi-witness cases have no such mechanism available.
Peters' 64%-remote finding is the single strongest empirical driver of this thesis — classical skeptical explanations require proximity to the dying brain.
Cross-cultural consistency is an independent line of evidence against the 'cultural expectation' skeptical model.
Child cases remove the 'learned cultural script' variable that most skeptical arguments rely on.
